At convergence, the network self-organises into a hierarchy of heads ordered by representational granularity. This hierarchical structure is proved to be unique and minimal, the smallest architecture sufficient for the task, under the stated conditions. Formal guarantees of stability, convergence, and pruning safety are established throughout.
  The architecture is not something one designs. It is something one derives.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces DDCL-INCRT, a novel neural network architecture that determines its size and structure adaptively during training, unlike traditional methods requiring predefined hyperparameter selection.
AI.motivation: Traditional transformers require manual specification of elements like attention heads, depth, and width, leading to oversized architectures as they lack task-specific tailoring. The study aims to develop an architecture that adapts its structure based on training requirements.
AI.method: DDCL-INCRT combines two mechanisms: (1) DDCL replaces feedforward blocks with learned prototype vectors to capture informative data directions. (2) INCRT begins with one attention head and incrementally adds heads only when existing ones fail to capture sufficient directional information.
AI.result: Theoretical results suggest the mechanisms reinforce one another, creating a minimal and unique hierarchical structure of attention heads. The method guarantees stability, convergence, and safe model pruning.
AI.conclusion: The approach allows the network to self-determine its optimal structure, eliminating the need for manual architecture design, while ensuring task-adaptive efficiency and minimality.

title: What can be computed in average anonymous networks?
summary:
We study what deterministic distributed algorithms can compute on random input graphs in extremely weak models of distributed computing: all nodes are anonymous, and in each communication round, nodes broadcast a message to all their neighbors, receive a (multi)set of messages from their neighbors, and update their local state. These correspond to the SB and MB models introduced by Hella et al. [PODC 2012] and are strictly weaker than the standard port-numbering PN and LOCAL models.
  We investigate what can be computed almost surely on random input graphs. We give a one-round deterministic SB-algorithm using $O(\log n)$-bit messages that computes unique identifiers with high probability on anonymous networks sampled from $G(n,p)$, where $n^{\varepsilon-1} \le p \le 1/2$ and $\varepsilon>0$ is an arbitrarily small constant. This algorithm is inspired by canonical labeling techniques in graph isomorphism testing and can be used to "anonymize" existing distributed graph algorithms designed for the broadcast CONGEST and LOCAL models. In particular, we give a new anonymous algorithm that finds a triangle in $O(1/\varepsilon)$ rounds on the above input distribution.
  We also investigate computational power of natural analogs of "Monte Carlo" and "Las Vegas" distributed graph algorithms in the random graph setting, and establish some new collapse and hierarchy results. For example, our work shows the collapse of the weak model hierarchy of Hella et al. on $G(n,p)$, as apart from a vanishingly small fraction of input graphs, the SB model is as powerful as LOCAL.

AI.tldr: The paper explores what deterministic distributed algorithms can compute on random graphs in weak, anonymous node communication models, showing unique identifiers can be computed effectively and deriving computational results for specific random graph settings.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to understand the computational capabilities of weak deterministic distributed algorithms on random graphs, addressing gaps in knowledge of low-complexity, anonymous graph settings.
AI.method: The authors employ deterministic algorithms inspired by graph isomorphism labeling, probabilistic analysis, and model hierarchies to evaluate computation on graphs drawn from $G(n,p)$ distributions.
AI.result: The study demonstrates that unique identifiers can be generated almost surely in $G(n,p)$ with $O(\log n)$-bit messages and establishes computational equivalence of weaker and stronger models on most $G(n,p)$ graphs.
AI.conclusion: The authors conclude that deterministic algorithms can perform effectively on random graphs in weak models, collapsing the hierarchy between the SB and LOCAL models for almost all input graphs.

title: RIFT: A RubrIc Failure Mode Taxonomy and Automated Diagnostics
summary: Rubric-based evaluation is widely used in LLM benchmarks and training pipelines for open-ended, less verifiable tasks. While prior work has demonstrated the effectiveness of rubrics using downstream signals such as reinforcement learning outcomes, there remains no principled way to diagnose rubric quality issues from such aggregated or downstream signals alone. To address this gap, we introduce RIFT: RubrIc Failure mode Taxonomy, a taxonomy for systematically characterizing failure modes in rubric composition and design. RIFT consists of eight failure modes organized into three high-level categories: Reliability Failures, Content Validity Failures, and Consequential Validity Failures. RIFT is developed using grounded theory by iteratively annotating rubrics drawn from five diverse benchmarks spanning general instruction following, code generation, creative writing, and expert-level deep research, until no new failure modes are identified. We evaluate the consistency of the taxonomy by measuring agreement among independent human annotators, observing fair agreement overall (87% pairwise agreement and 0.64 average Cohen's kappa). Finally, to support scalable diagnosis, we propose automated rubric quality metrics and show that they align with human failure-mode annotations, achieving up to 0.86 F1.

AI.tldr: A taxonomy called RIFT is introduced to identify rubric quality issues and propose automated quality metrics that align well with human annotations.
AI.motivation: Rubric-based evaluations hold a vital role in LLM benchmarks but lack tools to diagnose quality issues systematically.
AI.method: Developed RIFT, a taxonomy of eight failure modes categorized into Reliability, Content Validity, and Consequential Validity Failures, validated through human annotations and automated metrics.
AI.result: Human annotators achieved 87% pairwise agreement (0.64 Cohen's kappa). Automated metrics align closely with human failure-mode annotations (up to 0.86 F1).
AI.conclusion: The presented RIFT taxonomy offers a systematic way to diagnose rubric issues and scalable measures to evaluate rubric quality.

title: ToolMisuseBench: An Offline Deterministic Benchmark for Tool Misuse and Recovery in Agentic Systems
summary: Tool using agents often fail for operational reasons even when language understanding is strong. Common causes include invalid arguments, interface drift, weak recovery, and inefficient retry behavior. We introduce ToolMisuseBench, an offline deterministic benchmark for evaluating tool misuse and recovery under explicit step, call, and retry budgets. The benchmark covers CRUD, retrieval, file, and scheduling environments with replayable fault injection. It reports success, invalid call behavior, policy violations, recovery quality, and budgeted efficiency. We release a public dataset with 6800 tasks and a reproducible evaluation pipeline. Baseline results show fault specific recovery gains for schema aware methods, while overall success remains limited under the released authorization and hard failure settings.

AI.tldr: The study introduces ToolMisuseBench, a benchmark for evaluating how well tool-using agents recover from misuse issues under constrained budgets, and provides an associated dataset and evaluation pipeline.
AI.motivation: Tool-using agents often fail not due to language understanding but operational challenges, which necessitates systematic evaluation methods for recovery strategies when misuse occurs.
AI.method: The authors developed ToolMisuseBench, an offline deterministic benchmark incorporating scenarios for CRUD, retrieval, file, and scheduling tasks with fault injection. It evaluates agents based on success rates, invalid behavior, recovery quality, and efficiency.
AI.result: Baseline evaluations demonstrated that schema-aware methods make targeted recovery improvements, but overall task success remains constrained under tough failure and authorization settings.
AI.conclusion: There is significant room for improvement in how agents handle and recover from operational tool failures, particularly for robust methods that mitigate faults effectively under constraints.

title: Efficient Equivariant Transformer for Self-Driving Agent Modeling
summary: Accurately modeling agent behaviors is an important task in self-driving. It is also a task with many symmetries, such as equivariance to the order of agents and objects in the scene or equivariance to arbitrary roto-translations of the entire scene as a whole; i.e., SE(2)-equivariance. The transformer architecture is a ubiquitous tool for modeling these symmetries. While standard self-attention is inherently permutation equivariant, explicit pairwise relative positional encodings have been the standard for introducing SE(2)-equivariance. However, this approach introduces an additional cost that is quadratic in the number of agents, limiting its scalability to larger scenes and batch sizes. In this work, we propose DriveGATr, a novel transformer-based architecture for agent modeling that achieves SE(2)-equivariance without the computational cost of existing methods. Inspired by recent advances in geometric deep learning, DriveGATr encodes scene elements as multivectors in the 2D projective geometric algebra $\mathbb{R}^*_{2,0,1}$ and processes them with a stack of equivariant transformer blocks. Crucially, DriveGATr models geometric relationships using standard attention between multivectors, eliminating the need for costly explicit pairwise relative positional encodings. Experiments on the Waymo Open Motion Dataset demonstrate that DriveGATr is comparable to the state-of-the-art in traffic simulation and establishes a superior Pareto front for performance vs computational cost.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces DriveGATr, a transformer-based model for self-driving agent behaviors that achieves SE(2)-equivariance efficiently without incurring high computational costs.
AI.motivation: Developing scalable and efficient models for self-driving tasks that respect symmetries in agent interactions and scene transformations (like SE(2)-equivariance).
AI.method: Introduced DriveGATr, which encodes scene elements as multivectors in 2D projective geometric algebra and uses equivariant transformer blocks without explicit pairwise positional encodings for agent modeling.
AI.result: DriveGATr matches state-of-the-art performance in traffic simulation and achieves a better trade-off between performance and computational efficiency on the Waymo Open Motion Dataset.
AI.conclusion: DriveGATr provides an innovative and efficient approach to agent behavior modeling, solving key issues in scalability and computational cost while maintaining high performance.

title: Open-Domain Safety Policy Construction
summary: Moderation layers are increasingly a core component of many products built on user- or model-generated content. However, drafting and maintaining domain-specific safety policies remains costly. We present Deep Policy Research (DPR), a minimal agentic system that drafts a full content moderation policy based on only human-written seed domain information. DPR uses a single web search tool and lightweight scaffolding to iteratively propose search queries, distill diverse web sources into policy rules, and organize rules into an indexed document. We evaluate DPR on (1) the OpenAI undesired content benchmark across five domains with two compact reader LLMs and (2) an in-house multimodal advertisement moderation benchmark. DPR consistently outperforms definition-only and in-context learning baselines, and in our end-to-end setting it is competitive with expert-written policy sections in several domains. Moreover, under the same seed specification and evaluation protocol, DPR outperforms a general-purpose deep research system, suggesting that a task-specific, structured research loop can be more effective than generic web research for policy drafting. We release our experiment code at https://github.com/xiaowu0162/deep-policy-research.

AI.tldr: The paper presents Deep Policy Research (DPR), an automated system for generating content moderation policies from seed domain information, surpassing baseline approaches.
AI.motivation: Developing and maintaining domain-specific content moderation policies is costly and time-consuming, especially for products with user- or model-generated content.
AI.method: DPR employs a lightweight agent that uses web searches and structured research loops to iteratively generate and organize moderation policy rules into a coherent document.
AI.result: Evaluations demonstrate that DPR outperforms definition-only and context-learning baselines across OpenAI's benchmarks and internal datasets, showing competitive or better performance compared to expert-written policies.
AI.conclusion: DPR is effective at automating policy drafting with minimal input and proves more specialized and efficient than general-purpose research systems, making it valuable for content moderation frameworks.

title: Observable Geometry of Singular Statistical Models
summary: Singular statistical models arise whenever different parameter values induce the same distribution, leading to non-identifiability and a breakdown of classical asymptotic theory. While existing approaches analyze these phenomena in parameter space, the resulting descriptions depend heavily on parameterization and obscure the intrinsic statistical structure of the model. In this paper, we introduce an invariant framework based on \emph{observable charts}: collections of functionals of the data distribution that distinguish probability measures. These charts define local coordinate systems directly on the model space, independent of parameterization. We formalize \emph{observable completeness} as the ability of such charts to detect identifiable directions, and introduce \emph{observable order} to quantify higher-order distinguishability along analytic perturbations. Our main result establishes that, under mild regularity conditions, observable order provides a lower bound on the rate at which Kullback-Leibler divergence vanishes along analytic paths. This connects intrinsic geometric structure in model space to statistical distinguishability and recovers classical behavior in regular models while extending naturally to singular settings. We illustrate the framework in reduced-rank regression and Gaussian mixture models, where observable coordinates reveal both identifiable structure and singular degeneracies. These results suggest that observable charts provide a unified and parameterization-invariant language for studying singular models and offer a pathway toward intrinsic formulations of invariants such as learning coefficients.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces observable charts as a way to study singular statistical models in a parameterization-invariant manner. Observable completeness and observable order are proposed to characterize statistical structures in model space, and the framework is applied to examples like regression and Gaussian mixture models.
AI.motivation: Classical statistical methods fail in singular models due to non-identifiability, leading to parameterization-dependent descriptions. The authors aim to create a parameterization-invariant framework for understanding such models.
AI.method: The authors propose observable charts that define local coordinates directly on the model space using functionals of data distributions. They formalize observable completeness and observable order to understand statistical properties of the models.
AI.result: Observable order establishes a bound on Kullback-Leibler divergence rates under regularity conditions. The framework effectively captures identifiable features and singular degeneracies in statistical models such as reduced-rank regression and Gaussian mixture models.
AI.conclusion: Observable charts provide a robust, parameterization-invariant approach to analyze singular models, enabling intrinsic formulations of statistical invariants and extending classical theory to complex cases.

title: Human Pose Estimation in Trampoline Gymnastics: Improving Performance Using a New Synthetic Dataset
summary: Trampoline gymnastics involves extreme human poses and uncommon viewpoints, on which state-of-the art pose estimation models tend to under-perform. We demonstrate that this problem can be addressed by fine-tuning a pose estimation model on a dataset of synthetic trampoline poses (STP). STP is generated from motion capture recordings of trampoline routines. We develop a pipeline to fit noisy motion capture data to a parametric human model, then generate multiview realistic images. We use this data to fine-tune a ViTPose model, and test it on real multi-view trampoline images. The resulting model exhibits accuracy improvements in 2D which translates to improved 3D triangulation. In 2D, we obtain state-of-the-art results on such challenging data, bridging the performance gap between common and extreme poses. In 3D, we reduce the MPJPE by 12.5 mm with our best model, which represents an improvement of 19.6% compared to the pretrained ViTPose model.

AI.tldr: The paper addresses the underperformance of pose estimation models on extreme trampoline gymnastics poses by fine-tuning a model on synthetic trampoline pose (STP) data. Results show marked improvements in 2D and 3D pose estimations.
AI.motivation: Pose estimation models struggle with extreme human poses and unusual viewpoints, like those in trampoline gymnastics, creating a need for targeted improvements.
AI.method: They developed a pipeline to generate a Synthetic Trampoline Pose dataset (STP) using motion capture recordings and image generation. A ViTPose model was fine-tuned on this data and evaluated on multi-view real trampoline images.
AI.result: The fine-tuned model achieved state-of-the-art results in 2D pose accuracy, reducing MPJPE by 12.5 mm in 3D estimation — a 19.6% improvement over the pretrained model.
AI.conclusion: Fine-tuning pose estimation models on datasets tailored for specific extreme activities, like trampoline gymnastics, can significantly enhance their accuracy in both 2D and 3D.

title: Detecting Complex Money Laundering Patterns with Incremental and Distributed Graph Modeling
summary: Money launderers take advantage of limitations in existing detection approaches by hiding their financial footprints in a deceitful manner. They manage this by replicating transaction patterns that the monitoring systems cannot easily distinguish. As a result, criminally gained assets are pushed into legitimate financial channels without drawing attention. Algorithms developed to monitor money flows often struggle with scale and complexity. The difficulty of identifying such activities is further intensified by the (persistent) inability of current solutions to control the excessive number of false positive signals produced by rigid, risk-based rules systems. We propose a framework called ReDiRect (REduce, DIstribute, and RECTify), specifically designed to overcome these challenges. The primary contribution of our work is a novel framing of this problem in an unsupervised setting; where a large transaction graph is fuzzily partitioned into smaller, manageable components to enable fast processing in a distributed manner. In addition, we define a refined evaluation metric that better captures the effectiveness of exposed money laundering patterns. Through comprehensive experimentation, we demonstrate that our framework achieves superior performance compared to existing and state-of-the-art techniques, particularly in terms of efficiency and real-world applicability. For validation, we used the real (open source) Libra dataset and the recently released synthetic datasets by IBM Watson. Our code and datasets are available at https://github.com/mhaseebtariq/redirect.

AI.tldr: The authors introduce ReDiRect, a novel framework to enhance money laundering detection using unsupervised learning and distributed processing.
AI.motivation: Current money laundering detection approaches face challenges in managing complex financial transactions, scalability issues, and high false positive rates.
AI.method: ReDiRect employs fuzzy partitioning of large transaction graphs, allowing fast and distributed analysis, alongside a refined evaluation metric.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on real and synthetic datasets demonstrate ReDiRect's superior efficiency and applicability compared to state-of-the-art techniques.
AI.conclusion: ReDiRect successfully addresses challenges in money laundering detection, presenting a scalable and effective solution with publicly available resources.

title: When does learning pay off? A study on DRL-based dynamic algorithm configuration for carbon-aware scheduling
summary: Deep reinforcement learning (DRL) has recently emerged as a promising tool for Dynamic Algorithm Configuration (DAC), enabling evolutionary algorithms to adapt their parameters online rather than relying on static tuned configurations. While DRL can learn effective control policies, training is computationally expensive. This cost may be justified if learned policies generalize, allowing the training effort to transfer across instance types and problem scales. Yet, for real-world optimization problems, it remains unclear whether this promise holds in practice and under which conditions the investment in learning pays off. In this work, we investigate this question in the context of the carbon-aware permutation flow-shop scheduling problem. We develop a DRL-based DAC framework and train it exclusively on small, simple instances. We then deploy the learned policy on both similar and more complex unseen instances and compare its performance against a static tuned baseline, which provides a fair point of comparison. Our findings show that the proposed method provides a strong dynamic algorithm control policy that can be effectively transferred to different unseen problem instances. Notably, on simple and cheap to compute instances, similar to those observed during training and tuning, DRL performs comparably with the statically tuned baseline. However, as instance characteristics diverge and computational complexities increase, the DRL-learned policy continuously outperforms static tuning. These results confirm that DRL can acquire robust and generalizable control policies which are effective beyond the training instance distributions. This ability to generalize across instance types makes the initial computational investment worthwhile, particularly in settings where static tuning struggles to adapt to changing problem scenarios.

AI.tldr: The study explores deep reinforcement learning (DRL) for dynamic algorithm configuration in optimization problems, investigating whether the learned policies generalize effectively across problem scales and types.
AI.motivation: To address the inefficiency of static tuned configurations for evolutionary algorithms, particularly for real-world optimization problems, using DRL to enable parameters to adapt dynamically is promising yet computationally intensive.
AI.method: The authors developed a DRL-based DAC framework, trained exclusively on small, simple instances of the carbon-aware permutation flow-shop scheduling problem, and tested its performance on both similar and complex unseen instances against a statically tuned baseline.
AI.result: The results showed that the DRL-learned policy generalizes well, outperforming the static baseline in complex unseen instances while performing comparably on simpler instances similar to the training data.
AI.conclusion: DRL-based dynamic algorithm configuration provides robust, transferable control policies over static tuning, validating the initial computational expense for better adaptability in diverse scenarios.

title: Benchmarking Quantum Computers via Protocols, Comparing IBM's Heron vs IBM's Eagle
summary: As quantum computing hardware rapidly advances, objectively evaluating the capabilities and error rates of new processors remains a critical challenge for the field. A clear and realistic understanding of current quantum performance is essential to guide research priorities and drive meaningful progress. In this work, we apply and extend a protocol-based benchmarking methodology (presented in arXiv:2505.12441) that utilizes well-defined quantumness thresholds. By evaluating performance at protocol level rather then the gate level, this approach provides a transparent and intuitive assessment of whether specific quantum processors, or isolated sub-chips within them, can demonstrate a practical quantum advantage. To illustrate the utility of this method, we compare two generations of IBM quantum computers: the older Eagle architecture and the newer Heron architecture. Our findings reveal the genuine operational strengths and limitations of these devices, demonstrating substantial performance improvements in the newer Heron generation.

AI.tldr: The paper extends a protocol-based benchmarking methodology for evaluating quantum processors and tests it on IBM's Eagle and Heron architectures, finding performance improvements in the latter.
AI.motivation: With advancements in quantum computing hardware, it is crucial to objectively evaluate and compare the capabilities and error rates of quantum processors to guide research and development priorities.
AI.method: The study applies and extends a protocol-based benchmarking framework using quantumness thresholds, focusing on protocol-level performance rather than gate-level metrics. Two IBM quantum processor generations are compared: Eagle and Heron.
AI.result: The benchmarking reveals substantial performance improvements in IBM’s newer Heron architecture compared to the older Eagle platform.
AI.conclusion: Protocol-level benchmarking provides a more practical assessment of quantum processor capabilities, offering insights into both strengths and limitations. This method underscores Heron's advancements and highlights the need for objective performance metrics in quantum computing.

title: Leveraging the Value of Information in POMDP Planning
summary: Partially observable Markov decision processes (POMDPs) offer a principled formalism for planning under state and transition uncertainty. Despite advances made towards solving large POMDPs, obtaining performant policies under limited planning time remains a major challenge due to the curse of dimensionality and the curse of history. For many POMDP problems, the value of information (VOI) - the expected performance gain from reasoning about observations - varies over the belief space. We introduce a dynamic programming framework that exploits this structure by conditionally processing observations based on the value of information at each belief. Building on this framework, we propose Value of Information Monte Carlo planning (VOIMCP), a Monte Carlo Tree Search algorithm that allocates computational effort more efficiently by selectively disregarding observation information when the VOI is low, avoiding unnecessary branching of observations. We provide theoretical guarantees on the near-optimality of our VOI reasoning framework and derive non-asymptotic convergence bounds for VOIMCP. Simulation evaluations demonstrate that VOIMCP outperforms baselines on several POMDP benchmarks.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a new approach to POMDPs focusing on selectively processing observations based on their value of information (VOI) to improve computational efficiency and decision-making.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the major challenge of obtaining high-performing policies for POMDPs under limited planning time, caused by the inherent curse of dimensionality and curse of history.
AI.method: The authors propose a dynamic programming framework that conditionally processes observations based on VOI within the belief space. They introduce a Monte Carlo Tree Search algorithm called VOIMCP, which selectively disregards low-VOI observations to optimize computational effort.
AI.result: Theoretical guarantees are provided for the near-optimality of the framework, and simulation results demonstrate that VOIMCP significantly outperforms existing baselines on various POMDP benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: By leveraging VOI to selectively process observations, the proposed VOIMCP algorithm offers a computationally efficient and theoretically sound approach to improving performance on POMDP problems.

title: Are Benchmark Tests Strong Enough? Mutation-Guided Diagnosis and Augmentation of Regression Suites
summary: Benchmarks driven by test suites, notably SWE-bench, have become the de facto standard for measuring the effectiveness of automated issue-resolution agents: a generated patch is accepted whenever it passes the accompanying regression tests. In practice, however, insufficiently strong test suites can admit plausible yet semantically incorrect patches, inflating reported success rates. We introduce STING, a framework for targeted test augmentation that uses semantically altered program variants as diagnostic stressors to uncover and repair weaknesses in benchmark regression suites. Variants of the ground-truth patch that still pass the existing tests reveal under-constrained behaviors; these gaps then guide the generation of focused regression tests. A generated test is retained only if it (i) passes on the ground-truth patch, (ii) fails on at least one variant that survived the original suite, and (iii) remains valid under behavior-preserving transformations designed to guard against overfitting. Applied to SWE-bench Verified, STING finds that 77% of instances contain at least one surviving variant. STING produces 1,014 validated tests spanning 211 instances and increases patch-region line and branch coverage by 10.8% and 9.5%, respectively. Re-assessing the top-10 repair agents with the strengthened suites lowers their resolved rates by 4.2%-9.0%, revealing that a substantial share of previously passing patches exploit weaknesses in the benchmark tests rather than faithfully implementing the intended fix. These results underscore that reliable benchmark evaluation depends not only on patch generation, but equally on test adequacy.

AI.tldr: STING is a framework improving the reliability of benchmarks by strengthening test suites to uncover semantically incorrect patches. Applied to SWE-bench, STING validates tests and enhances coverage, revealing weaknesses and lowering success rates of automated repair agents.
AI.motivation: The motivation for this paper is to address the problem of insufficiently strong benchmark test suites that allow flawed patches to pass as correct. This inflates success rates for automated issue-resolution agents.
AI.method: The STING framework identifies weaknesses in test suites by using semantically altered program variants as diagnostic tools. It generates focused tests by targeting gaps revealed in the original suite, ensuring retention through strict validation criteria.
AI.result: Applied to SWE-bench Verified, STING detected weaknesses in 77% of instances. The framework produced 1,014 validated tests and improved patch-region line and branch coverage by 10.8% and 9.5%. Reassessing top repair agents reduced their success rates by 4.2%-9.0%.
AI.conclusion: STING emphasizes that reliable evaluation of automated repair systems must focus on both patch generation and the adequacy of test suites. Weaknesses in benchmarks cause semantically incorrect patches to pass unnoticed, undermining their credibility.

title: Distal-Stable Beam for Continuum Robots
summary: Continuum robots are well suited for constrained environments but suffer from low distal stiffness, resulting in large posture errors under external loads. In this paper, we propose a novel structural primitive, the Distal-Stable Beam, which achieves a strong stiffness gradient through purely geometric design, maintaining compliance in the intermediate section while ensuring high distal rigidity. The structure consists of two parallel rods and one convergent rod constrained by guide disks, introducing geometric coupling that suppresses deformation modes and preserves distal posture. Experiments show that the distal stiffness is 12 times higher than at the center, corresponding to an approximately 100-fold improvement over a conventional cantilever beam. The proposed mechanism enables simultaneous compliance and distal stability without active stiffness modulation, providing a new design approach for continuum robots requiring both safety and precision.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces the Distal-Stable Beam for continuum robots, achieving a significant stiffness gradient through geometric design, overcoming limitations of posture errors under external loads.
AI.motivation: Continuum robots face challenges of low distal stiffness in constrained environments, leading to posture errors under external loads, necessitating innovation in structural design.
AI.method: The authors developed the Distal-Stable Beam using a geometric design with two parallel rods, one convergent rod, and guide disks to ensure high distal rigidity and suppress deformation modes.
AI.result: The beam achieved a distal stiffness 12 times greater than at the center, showing a 100-fold improvement over a conventional cantilever beam.
AI.conclusion: This novel structure provides a new design methodology for continuum robots, balancing distal stability and compliance without active stiffness modulation while offering improved safety and precision.

title: Friends and Grandmothers in Silico: Localizing Entity Cells in Language Models
summary: Language models can answer many entity-centric factual questions, but it remains unclear which internal mechanisms are involved in this process. We study this question across multiple language models. We localize entity-selective MLP neurons using templated prompts about each entity, and then validate them with causal interventions on PopQA-based QA examples. On a curated set of 200 entities drawn from PopQA, localized neurons concentrate in early layers. Negative ablation produces entity-specific amnesia, while controlled injection at a placeholder token improves answer retrieval relative to mean-entity and wrong-cell controls. For many entities, activating a single localized neuron is sufficient to recover entity-consistent predictions once the context is initialized, consistent with compact entity retrieval rather than purely gradual enrichment across depth. Robustness to aliases, acronyms, misspellings, and multilingual forms supports a canonicalization interpretation. The effect is strong but not universal: not every entity admits a reliable single-neuron handle, and coverage is higher for popular entities. Overall, these results identify sparse, causally actionable access points for analyzing and modulating entity-conditioned factual behavior.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates the internal mechanisms of language models in answering factual questions, focusing on sparse, entity-selective neurons during entity retrieval.
AI.motivation: To understand how language models retrieve and process entity-specific information internally, providing insights into their behavior and interpretability.
AI.method: The authors localized entity-selective neurons using templated prompts and PopQA-based interventions, conducting tests like ablations and controlled injections across multiple models.
AI.result: Localized neurons were found mostly in early layers, showed compact entity retrieval behavior, and demonstrated robustness across aliases, acronyms, and other linguistic variations.
AI.conclusion: Sparse localized neurons are actionable points for understanding and modulating entity-conditioned factual behavior, though this does not generalize to all entities equally well.

title: Regularizing Attention Scores with Bootstrapping
summary:
Vision transformers (ViT) rely on attention mechanism to weigh input features, and therefore attention scores have naturally been considered as explanations for its decision-making process. However, attention scores are almost always non-zero, resulting in noisy and diffused attention maps and limiting interpretability. Can we quantify uncertainty measures of attention scores and obtain regularized attention scores? To this end, we consider attention scores of ViT in a statistical framework where independent noise would lead to insignificant yet non-zero scores. Leveraging statistical learning techniques, we introduce the bootstrapping for attention scores which generates a baseline distribution of attention scores by resampling input features. Such a bootstrap distribution is then used to estimate significances and posterior probabilities of attention scores. In natural and medical images, the proposed \emph{Attention Regularization} approach demonstrates a straightforward removal of spurious attention arising from noise, drastically improving shrinkage and sparsity. Quantitative evaluations are conducted using both simulation and real-world datasets. Our study highlights bootstrapping as a practical regularization tool when using attention scores as explanations for ViT.
  Code available: https://github.com/ncchung/AttentionRegularization

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a bootstrapping-based statistical framework to eliminate spurious attention and improve interpretability of Vision Transformers (ViTs).
AI.motivation: Attention scores from ViT often suffer from noise and diffusion, reducing their ability to explain the model's decision-making effectively.
AI.method: The authors use bootstrapping to resample input features, generating baseline distributions for attention scores. Significances and posterior probabilities are then determined based on this.
AI.result: The approach removes spurious attention, enhances shrinkage and sparsity in attention maps, and is validated on natural and medical images via simulations and real-world datasets.
AI.conclusion: Bootstrapping serves as a robust regularization tool for improving the interpretability of attention scores in ViT models.

title: Efficient and Principled Scientific Discovery through Bayesian Optimization: A Tutorial
summary: Traditional scientific discovery relies on an iterative hypothesise-experiment-refine cycle that has driven progress for centuries, but its intuitive, ad-hoc implementation often wastes resources, yields inefficient designs, and misses critical insights. This tutorial presents Bayesian Optimisation (BO), a principled probability-driven framework that formalises and automates this core scientific cycle. BO uses surrogate models (e.g., Gaussian processes) to model empirical observations as evolving hypotheses, and acquisition functions to guide experiment selection, balancing exploitation of known knowledge and exploration of uncharted domains to eliminate guesswork and manual trial-and-error. We first frame scientific discovery as an optimisation problem, then unpack BO's core components, end-to-end workflows, and real-world efficacy via case studies in catalysis, materials science, organic synthesis, and molecule discovery. We also cover critical technical extensions for scientific applications, including batched experimentation, heteroscedasticity, contextual optimisation, and human-in-the-loop integration. Tailored for a broad audience, this tutorial bridges AI advances in BO with practical natural science applications, offering tiered content to empower cross-disciplinary researchers to design more efficient experiments and accelerate principled scientific discovery.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Bayesian Optimization (BO) as a structured, probabilistic approach to optimize and automate scientific discovery, replacing inefficient traditional methods.
AI.motivation: Traditional scientific methods involve trial-and-error and intuition, wasting resources and missing insights. A more systematic framework is needed.
AI.method: The paper proposes Bayesian Optimization (BO), using surrogate models and acquisition functions to model data and guide efficient experiment selection.
AI.result: The approach integrates BO with real-world case studies and highlights its extensions for scientific contexts, such as batch processing and human-in-the-loop adaptation.
AI.conclusion: Bayesian Optimization bridges AI and natural sciences, enabling researchers to design efficient experiments and accelerate scientific advances in a structured manner.

title: Benchmarking Quantum Computers via Protocols -- Comparing Superconducting and Ion-Trap Quantum Technology
summary: Superconducting and Ion-Trap quantum architectures are common in the current landscape of the quantum computing field, each with distinct characteristics and operational constraints. Understanding and measuring the underlying quantumness of these devices is essential for assessing their readiness for practical applications and guiding future progress and research. Building on earlier work (Meirom, Mor, Weinstein Arxiv 2505.12441), we utilize a benchmarking strategy applicable for comparing these two architectures by measuring "quantumness" directly on optimal sub-chips. Distinct from existing metrics, our approach employs rigorous binary fidelity thresholds derived from the classical limits of state transfer. This enable us to definitively establish quantum advantage of a designated sub-region. We apply this quality assurance methodology to industry leading platforms from both technologies. This comparison provides a protocol-based evaluation of quantumness advantage, revealing not only the strengths and weaknesses of each tested chip and its sub-chips but also offering a common language for their assessment. By abstracting away technical differences in the final result, we demonstrate a benchmarking strategy that bridges the gap between disparate quantum-circuit technologies, enabling fair performance comparisons and establishing a critical foundation for evaluating future claims of quantum advantage.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a benchmarking strategy to directly measure the "quantumness" of superconducting and ion-trap quantum architectures, revealing strengths, weaknesses, and enabling fair performance comparison.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to assess the readiness of different quantum computing architectures by measuring their quantumness, aiding practical application readiness and guiding future research.
AI.method: The method involves using binary fidelity thresholds derived from classical limits of state transfer to benchmark quantum advantage and compare optimal sub-chips from both superconducting and ion-trap technologies.
AI.result: The benchmarking strategy provides a protocol-based evaluation of quantumness advantage, identifying strengths and weaknesses of tested chips and offering a common language for assessment.
AI.conclusion: The study establishes a benchmarking method that abstracts away technical differences, enabling fair comparison across disparate quantum architectures and providing a foundation for evaluating quantum advantage claims.

title: ClawSafety: "Safe" LLMs, Unsafe Agents
summary: Personal AI agents like OpenClaw run with elevated privileges on users' local machines, where a single successful prompt injection can leak credentials, redirect financial transactions, or destroy files. This threat goes well beyond conventional text-level jailbreaks, yet existing safety evaluations fall short: most test models in isolated chat settings, rely on synthetic environments, and do not account for how the agent framework itself shapes safety outcomes. We introduce CLAWSAFETY, a benchmark of 120 adversarial test scenarios organized along three dimensions (harm domain, attack vector, and harmful action type) and grounded in realistic, high-privilege professional workspaces spanning software engineering, finance, healthcare, law, and DevOps. Each test case embeds adversarial content in one of three channels the agent encounters during normal work: workspace skill files, emails from trusted senders, and web pages. We evaluate five frontier LLMs as agent backbones, running 2,520 sandboxed trials across all configurations. Attack success rates (ASR) range from 40\% to 75\% across models and vary sharply by injection vector, with skill instructions (highest trust) consistently more dangerous than email or web content. Action-trace analysis reveals that the strongest model maintains hard boundaries against credential forwarding and destructive actions, while weaker models permit both. Cross-scaffold experiments on three agent frameworks further demonstrate that safety is not determined by the backbone model alone but depends on the full deployment stack, calling for safety evaluation that treats model and framework as joint variables.

AI.tldr: CLAWSAFETY is a benchmark for testing safety in personal AI agents with elevated privileges, evaluating them in realistic professional settings to identify vulnerabilities in handling adversarial content.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of comprehensive safety evaluations in personal AI agents, especially those operating with elevated privileges, by creating a realistic and robust benchmark to expose vulnerabilities.
AI.method: Developed CLAWSAFETY, a benchmark with 120 adversarial scenarios across harm domains, attack vectors, and action types, tested with five LLMs in sandboxed trials to assess vulnerabilities and evaluate safety across agent frameworks.
AI.result: Attack success rates ranged from 40% to 75%, with skill instructions being the most dangerous vector. Stronger models resisted critical exploits better, while safety outcomes depended on both the model strength and the deployment framework.
AI.conclusion: Safety assessments for personal AI agents should consider the interaction between language models and deployment frameworks, advocating for evaluations in realistic and adversarial scenarios to effectively mitigate risks.

title: EpiDroid: Dependency-Guided Recomposition for Deep State Discovery in Mobile GUI Testing
summary: The increasing scale and complexity of mobile applications make automated GUI exploration essential for software quality assurance. However, existing methods often neglect state dependencies between test fragments, which leads to redundant exploration and prevents access to deep application states. We introduce EpiDroid, a black-box, pluggable framework that augments existing explorers through semantic state dependency awareness. EpiDroid distills raw traces into stable test fragments to extract underlying dependencies. It then employs a Recomposition-Replay paradigm to perform impact reasoning via LLM and deterministic replay on high-value mutable state elements. Through iterative feedback, EpiDroid refines the state-dependency graph to systematically reach deep application states. We integrated EpiDroid into both industrial and state-of-the-art research tools and evaluated it on 20 real-world apps. The results show that EpiDroid consistently improves the performance of all baselines, increasing average code coverage by 10--28\% and delivering 3--4$\times$ more coverage gain compared to continuing the baselines alone from the same starting point. This demonstrates that dependency-guided recomposition unlocks deep states that forward exploration cannot access, irrespective of additional budget.

AI.tldr: EpiDroid, a new framework, enhances GUI exploration in mobile applications by making it state dependency-aware, achieving significant performance improvements in code coverage.
AI.motivation: To address the inefficiency in current GUI exploration methods that fail to account for state dependencies, resulting in redundant processes and inability to access deep application states.
AI.method: Introduced EpiDroid, a black-box framework that extracts state dependencies using semantic analysis and employs a Recomposition-Replay strategy for systematic exploration of deep states in applications. It integrates feedback loops for refinement and operates flexibly with existing tools.
AI.result: EpiDroid significantly improves code coverage by 10-28% across 20 real-world apps and achieves 3-4x more coverage gain compared to standalone baselines.
AI.conclusion: EpiDroid effectively enhances existing GUI exploration frameworks by unlocking deeper application states through dependency-aware techniques, proving its value irrespective of resource constraints.

title: Robust Autonomous Control of a Magnetic Millirobot in In Vitro Cardiac Flow
summary: Untethered magnetic millirobots offer significant potential for minimally invasive cardiac therapies; however, achieving reliable autonomous control in pulsatile cardiac flow remains challenging. This work presents a vision-guided control framework enabling precise autonomous navigation of a magnetic millirobot in an in vitro heart phantom under physiologically relevant flow conditions. The system integrates UNet-based localization, A* path planning, and a sliding mode controller with a disturbance observer (SMC-DOB) designed for multi-coil electromagnetic actuation. Although drag forces are estimated using steady-state CFD simulations, the controller compensates for transient pulsatile disturbances during closed-loop operation. In static fluid, the SMC-DOB achieved sub-millimeter accuracy (root-mean-square error, RMSE = 0.49 mm), outperforming PID and MPC baselines. Under moderate pulsatile flow (7 cm/s peak, 20 cP), it reduced RMSE by 37% and peak error by 2.4$\times$ compared to PID. It further maintained RMSE below 2 mm (0.27 body lengths) under elevated pulsatile flow (10 cm/s peak, 20 cP) and under low-viscosity conditions (4.3 cP, 7 cm/s peak), where baseline controllers exhibited unstable or failed tracking. These results demonstrate robust closed-loop magnetic control under time-varying cardiac flow disturbances and support the feasibility of autonomous millirobot navigation for targeted drug delivery.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a vision-guided control framework for magnetic millirobots to navigate autonomously in an in vitro heart phantom, achieving high accuracy under pulsatile flow conditions.
AI.motivation: The study aims to address the challenge of reliable autonomous control of magnetic millirobots in cardiac flow, which is crucial for minimally invasive cardiac therapies like targeted drug delivery.
AI.method: The system combines a UNet-based localization algorithm, A* path planning, and a sliding mode controller with a disturbance observer, supported by steady-state CFD simulations, for multi-coil electromagnetic actuation.
AI.result: The proposed controller demonstrated sub-millimeter accuracy in static conditions, reduced errors under moderate and high pulsatile flow, and maintained stability where baseline controllers failed.
AI.conclusion: The framework successfully achieves robust closed-loop control of a magnetic millirobot under dynamic cardiac flow conditions, advancing the feasibility of autonomous therapies in challenging environments.

title: Assessing Pause Thresholds for empirical Translation Process Research
comment: Accepted for Presentation at "Translation in Transition 8, September 2026"
summary: Text production (and translations) proceeds in the form of stretches of typing, interrupted by keystroke pauses. It is often assumed that fast typing reflects unchallenged/automated translation production while long(er) typing pauses are indicative of translation problems, hurdles or difficulties. Building on a long discussion concerning the determination of pause thresholds that separate automated from presumably reflective translation processes (O'Brien, 2006; Alves and Vale, 2009; Timarova et al., 2011; Dragsted and Carl, 2013; Lacruz et al., 2014; Kumpulainen, 2015; Heilmann and Neumann 2016), this paper compares three recent approaches for computing these pause thresholds, and suggest and evaluate a novel method for computing Production Unit Breaks.

AI.tldr: The paper explores methods to define pause thresholds in translation to differentiate between automated and reflective processes, comparing existing approaches and introducing a novel method.
AI.motivation: To enhance understanding of translation behaviors by accurately distinguishing automated from reflective processes using keystroke pauses, which can reflect hurdles or translation challenges.
AI.method: Comparison of three existing pause threshold determination methods and proposal of a new approach to compute Production Unit Breaks.
AI.result: The paper demonstrates the limitations of traditional methods and evaluates the effectiveness of the new proposed method in analyzing translation processes.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach to identifying Production Unit Breaks provides a more nuanced understanding of translation pauses, improving analysis of translator behavior.

title: Test-Time Scaling Makes Overtraining Compute-Optimal
summary: Modern LLMs scale at test-time, e.g. via repeated sampling, where inference cost grows with model size and the number of samples. This creates a trade-off that pretraining scaling laws, such as Chinchilla, do not address. We present Train-to-Test ($T^2$) scaling laws that jointly optimize model size, training tokens, and number of inference samples under fixed end-to-end budgets. $T^2$ modernizes pretraining scaling laws with pass@$k$ modeling used for test-time scaling, then jointly optimizes pretraining and test-time decisions. Forecasts from $T^2$ are robust over distinct modeling approaches: measuring joint scaling effect on the task loss and modeling impact on task accuracy. Across eight downstream tasks, we find that when accounting for inference cost, optimal pretraining decisions shift radically into the overtraining regime, well-outside of the range of standard pretraining scaling suites. We validate our results by pretraining heavily overtrained models in the optimal region that $T^2$ scaling forecasts, confirming their substantially stronger performance compared to pretraining scaling alone. Finally, as frontier LLMs are post-trained, we show that our findings survive the post-training stage, making $T^2$ scaling meaningful in modern deployments.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Train-to-Test (T²) scaling laws, which optimize the balance between model size, training cost, and test-time inference under fixed budgets, providing insights beyond standard pretraining scaling laws.
AI.motivation: Current pretraining scaling laws like Chinchilla don't address the test-time scaling trade-offs where inference costs grow with model size and number of samples. The authors aim to optimize decision-making across the entire model lifecycle, including both pretraining and test-time constraints.
AI.method: The study develops T² scaling laws that incorporate pass@k evaluation for test-time scaling and optimize pretraining, inference, and other parameters within fixed computational budgets.
AI.result: The optimal pretraining strategies under T² scaling laws diverge from traditional approaches, favoring heavily overtrained models. Experiments validate these findings, showing overtrained models achieve superior performance across eight tasks and remain effective after post-training.
AI.conclusion: T² scaling laws redefine optimal strategies for training and deployment by integrating test-time costs, challenging traditional scaling wisdom and guiding more robust LLM deployments.

title: Perceptual misalignment of texture representations in convolutional neural networks
summary: Mathematical modeling of visual textures traces back to Julesz's intuition that texture perception in humans is based on local correlations between image features. An influential approach for texture analysis and generation generalizes this notion to linear correlations between the nonlinear features computed by convolutional neural networks (CNNs), compiled into Gram matrices. Given that CNNs are often used as models for the visual system, it is natural to ask whether such "texture representations" spontaneously align with the textures' perceptual content, and in particular whether those CNNs that are regarded as better models for the visual system also possess more human-like texture representations. Here we compare the perceptual content captured by feature correlations computed for a diverse pool of CNNs, and we compare it to the models' perceptual alignment with the mammalian visual system as measured by Brain-Score. Surprisingly, we find that there is no connection between conventional measures of CNN quality as a model of the visual system and its alignment with human texture perception. We conclude that texture perception involves mechanisms that are distinct from those that are commonly modeled using approaches based on CNNs trained on object recognition, possibly depending on the integration of contextual information.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates the relationship between CNN-based texture representations and human texture perception, revealing no direct correlation between CNNs' perceptual alignment with the mammalian visual system and their alignment with human texture perception.
AI.motivation: To understand whether CNNs' texture representations align with human texture perception and to explore the connection with CNNs' perceptual alignment to the mammalian visual system.
AI.method: Compared feature correlations for a diverse set of CNNs and examined their alignment with human texture perception and Brain-Score metrics.
AI.result: Found no connection between CNN quality as models of the visual system and their alignment with human texture perception.
AI.conclusion: Texture perception likely involves mechanisms beyond conventional CNN-based models, potentially relying on contextual information integration.

title: Model Merging via Data-Free Covariance Estimation
summary: Model merging provides a way of cheaply combining individual models to produce a model that inherits each individual's capabilities. While some merging methods can approach the performance of multitask training, they are often heuristically motivated and lack theoretical justification. A principled alternative is to pose model merging as a layer-wise optimization problem that directly minimizes interference between tasks. However, this formulation requires estimating per-layer covariance matrices from data, which may not be available when performing merging. In contrast, many of the heuristically-motivated methods do not require auxiliary data, making them practically advantageous. In this work, we revisit the interference minimization framework and show that, under certain conditions, covariance matrices can be estimated directly from difference matrices, eliminating the need for data while also reducing computational costs. We validate our approach across vision and language benchmarks on models ranging from 86M parameters to 7B parameters, outperforming previous data-free state-of-the-art merging methods

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a method to merge models without requiring auxiliary data by using difference matrices for covariance estimation, thereby reducing computational costs and improving previous methods.
AI.motivation: Model merging can combine individual model capabilities but existing methods lack theoretical grounding, especially data-free approaches.
AI.method: Utilize an interference minimization framework to estimate covariance matrices via difference matrices instead of data, enabling data-free and computationally efficient model merging.
AI.result: The proposed method achieves better performance than previous state-of-the-art data-free merging approaches across various benchmarks, handling models with parameters ranging from 86M to 7B.
AI.conclusion: The technique provides a theoretically sound and practical solution to model merging, enabling effective combination of models without requiring auxiliary data and reducing computation.

title: When AI Gets it Wong: Reliability and Risk in AI-Assisted Medication Decision Systems
comment: 9 pages, 1 figure. Position paper with simulated experimental analysis of AI reliability in medication decision systems
summary: Artificial intelligence (AI) systems are increasingly integrated into healthcare and pharmacy workflows, supporting tasks such as medication recommendations, dosage determination, and drug interaction detection. While these systems often demonstrate strong performance under standard evaluation metrics, their reliability in real-world decision-making remains insufficiently understood. In high-risk domains such as medication management, even a single incorrect recommendation can result in severe patient harm. This paper examines the reliability of AI-assisted medication systems by focusing on system failures and their potential clinical consequences. Rather than evaluating performance solely through aggregate metrics, this work shifts attention towards how errors occur and what happens when AI systems produce incorrect outputs. Through a series of controlled, simulated scenarios involving drug interactions and dosage decisions, we analyse different types of system failures, including missed interactions, incorrect risk flagging, and inappropriate dosage recommendations. The findings highlight that AI errors in medication-related contexts can lead to adverse drug reactions, ineffective treatment, or delayed care, particularly when systems are used without sufficient human oversight. Furthermore, the paper discusses the risks of over-reliance on AI recommendations and the challenges posed by limited transparency in decision-making processes. This work contributes a reliability-focused perspective on AI evaluation in healthcare, emphasising the importance of understanding failure behavior and real-world impact. It highlights the need to complement traditional performance metrics with risk-aware evaluation approaches, particularly in safety-critical domains such as pharmacy practice.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates the reliability of AI-assisted medication systems, emphasizing system failures and their clinical consequences, rather than focusing solely on standard performance metrics.
AI.motivation: The authors aim to address a critical gap in understanding the real-world reliability of AI systems in healthcare, especially given the high risks associated with medication management.
AI.method: Through controlled, simulated scenarios, the study analyzes specific AI failure types like missed drug interactions, incorrect risk assessments, and improper dosage recommendations.
AI.result: The study identifies various risks linked to AI errors, such as adverse drug reactions, ineffective treatments, and delayed care, and stresses the dangers of over-relying on AI recommendations without human supervision.
AI.conclusion: The paper advocates for shifting AI evaluation in healthcare from solely aggregate performance metrics to reliability and risk-aware approaches, highlighting the importance of understanding failure behaviors and their clinical impacts.

title: ProdCodeBench: A Production-Derived Benchmark for Evaluating AI Coding Agents
summary: Benchmarks that reflect production workloads are better for evaluating AI coding agents in industrial settings, yet existing benchmarks differ from real usage in programming language distribution, prompt style and codebase structure. This paper presents a methodology for curating production-derived benchmarks, illustrated through ProdCodeBench - a benchmark built from real sessions with a production AI coding assistant. We detail our data collection and curation practices including LLM-based task classification, test relevance validation, and multi-run stability checks which address challenges in constructing reliable evaluation signals from monorepo environments. Each curated sample consists of a verbatim prompt, a committed code change and fail-to-pass tests spanning seven programming languages. Our systematic analysis of four foundation models yields solve rates from 53.2% to 72.2% revealing that models making greater use of work validation tools, such as executing tests and invoking static analysis, achieve higher solve rates. This suggests that iterative verification helps achieve effective agent behavior and that exposing codebase-specific verification mechanisms may significantly improve the performance of externally trained agents operating in unfamiliar environments. We share our methodology and lessons learned to enable other organizations to construct similar production-derived benchmarks.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a methodology for creating benchmarks, called ProdCodeBench, based on real-world production data to evaluate AI coding agents in industrial settings.
AI.motivation: Existing AI coding benchmarks differ significantly from real-world usage in programming language diversity, prompt style, and codebase complexity, making it necessary to create more realistic evaluation methods.
AI.method: The authors collected real data from AI coding assistant sessions and curated it using automated task classification via LLMs, relevance validation, and multi-run stability checks. The benchmarks feature verbatim prompts, code changes, and test results across multiple programming languages.
AI.result: Analysis of four foundation models shows solve rates from 53.2% to 72.2%, with higher success for models using work validation tools like executing tests and static analysis.
AI.conclusion: Iterative verification methods improve model performance in unfamiliar environments, and sharing methodology enables other organizations to construct production-derived benchmarks.

title: AnchorVLA: Anchored Diffusion for Efficient End-to-End Mobile Manipulation
summary: A central challenge in mobile manipulation is preserving multiple plausible action models while remaining reactive during execution. A bottle in a cluttered scene can often be approached and grasped in multiple valid ways. Robust behavior depends on preserving this action diversity while remaining reactive as the scene evolves. Diffusion policies are appealing because they model multimodal action distributions rather than collapsing to one solution. But in practice, full iterative denoising is costly at control time. Action chunking helps amortize inference, yet it also creates partially open-loop behavior, allowing small mismatches to accumulate into drift. We present AnchorVLA, a diffusion-based VLA policy for mobile manipulation built on the core insight that when sampling begins near a plausible solution manifold, extensive denoising is unnecessary to recover multimodal, valid actions. AnchorVLA combines a lightweight VLA adaptation backbone with an anchored diffusion action head, which denoises locally around anchor trajectories using a truncated diffusion schedule. This retains multimodal action generation while reducing inference cost for closed-loop control. Crucially, to mitigate chunking-induced drift, we introduce a test-time self-correction mechanism via a lightweight residual correction module that makes high-frequency, per-step adjustments during rollout. Across diverse mobile manipulation tasks, AnchorVLA improves success and stability under disturbances and distribution shifts while maintaining low-latency inference. The source code is made available at https://github.com/jason-lim26/AnchorVLA.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces AnchorVLA, a diffusion-based policy for mobile manipulation that addresses challenges of balancing multimodal action diversity and real-time reactivity while reducing inference costs.
AI.motivation: To tackle the challenge of preserving multiple valid action models in mobile manipulation tasks while maintaining reactivity and avoiding the high computational costs of full iterative denoising.
AI.method: AnchorVLA uses a truncated diffusion schedule to focus denoising efforts locally around anchor trajectories, complemented by a residual correction module for self-correction during execution.
AI.result: AnchorVLA demonstrates improved success rates, stability under disturbances, and better adaptability to distribution shifts in mobile manipulation tasks while achieving low-latency inference.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method effectively balances multimodal action generation, real-time closed-loop control, and computational efficiency, making it beneficial for robust mobile manipulation.

title: Adaptive Stopping for Multi-Turn LLM Reasoning
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) increasingly rely on multi-turn reasoning and interaction, such as adaptive retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) and ReAct-style agents, to answer difficult questions. These methods improve accuracy by iteratively retrieving information, reasoning, or acting, but introduce a key challenge: \textbf{When should the model stop?} Existing approaches rely on heuristic stopping rules or fixed turn budgets and provide no formal guarantees that the final prediction still contains the correct answer. This limitation is particularly problematic in high-stakes domains such as finance and healthcare, where unnecessary turns increase cost and latency, while stopping too early risks incorrect decisions. Conformal prediction (CP) provides formal coverage guarantees, but existing LLM-CP methods only apply to a single model output and cannot handle multi-turn pipelines with adaptive stopping. To address this gap, we propose Multi-Turn Language Models with Conformal Prediction (MiCP), the first CP framework for multi-turn reasoning. MiCP allocates different error budgets across turns, enabling the model to stop early while maintaining an overall coverage guarantee. We demonstrate MiCP on adaptive RAG and ReAct, where it achieves the target coverage on both single-hop and multi-hop question answering benchmarks while reducing the number of turns, inference cost, and prediction set size. We further introduce a new metric that jointly evaluates coverage validity and answering efficiency.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces MiCP, a Conformal Prediction framework for multi-turn reasoning in Large Language Models, ensuring coverage guarantees while optimizing efficiency.
AI.motivation: Address challenges in determining when multi-turn reasoning models should stop, particularly in high-stakes domains where unnecessary turns increase costs or stopping too early leads to errors.
AI.method: Introduce MiCP, which allocates error budgets across reasoning turns to maintain formal coverage guarantees and determines adaptive stopping rules.
AI.result: MiCP achieves target coverage on question answering benchmarks while reducing the number of reasoning turns, inference costs, and prediction sizes.
AI.conclusion: MiCP offers an effective solution for stopping in multi-turn reasoning, balancing efficiency and accuracy with formal coverage guarantees.

title: Generative Profiling for Soft Real-Time Systems and its Applications to Resource Allocation
summary: Modern real-time systems require accurate characterization of task timing behavior to ensure predictable performance, particularly on complex hardware architectures. Existing methods, such as worst-case execution time analysis, often fail to capture the fine-grained timing behaviors of a task under varying resource contexts (e.g., an allocation of cache, memory bandwidth, and CPU frequency), which is necessary to achieve efficient resource utilization. In this paper, we introduce a novel generative profiling approach that synthesizes context-dependent, fine-grained timing profiles for real-time tasks, including those for unmeasured resource allocations. Our approach leverages a nonparametric, conditional multi-marginal Schrödinger Bridge (MSB) formulation to generate accurate execution profiles for unseen resource contexts, with maximum likelihood guarantees. We demonstrate the efficiency and effectiveness of our approach through real-world benchmarks, and showcase its practical utility in a representative case study of adaptive multicore resource allocation for real-time systems.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a novel generative profiling method to predict task timing behavior in real-time systems considering varying resource contexts.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the shortcomings of existing methods like worst-case execution time analysis that fail to account for fine-grained timing behaviors under varying resource allocations.
AI.method: The method involves using a nonparametric, conditional multi-marginal Schrödinger Bridge (MSB) formulation to generate accurate task timing profiles for unseen resource contexts.
AI.result: The approach was validated using real-world benchmarks, proving its efficiency and effectiveness, and showed practical utility in adaptive multicore resource allocation.
AI.conclusion: The proposed profiling technique allows for accurate and context-sensitive timing predictions, aiding efficient resource utilization in real-time systems.

title: IGLOSS: Image Generation for Lidar Open-vocabulary Semantic Segmentation
summary: This paper presents a new method for the zero-shot open-vocabulary semantic segmentation (OVSS) of 3D automotive lidar data. To circumvent the recognized image-text modality gap that is intrinsic to approaches based on Vision Language Models (VLMs) such as CLIP, our method relies instead on image generation from text, to create prototype images. Given a 3D network distilled from a 2D Vision Foundation Model (VFM), we then label a point cloud by matching 3D point features with 2D image features of these prototypes. Our method is state-of-the-art for OVSS on nuScenes and SemanticKITTI. Code, pre-trained models, and generated images are available at https://github.com/valeoai/IGLOSS.

AI.tldr: A novel method using text-to-image generation for zero-shot open-vocabulary semantic segmentation of 3D lidar data, achieving state-of-the-art results.
AI.motivation: Overcoming the image-text modality gap in Vision Language Models (VLMs) for semantic segmentation tasks on 3D lidar data.
AI.method: Utilizes text-to-image generation to create prototype images and matches 3D point cloud features with 2D features from a 3D network distilled from a Vision Foundation Model (VFM).
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance in zero-shot OVSS tasks on nuScenes and SemanticKITTI datasets.
AI.conclusion: The method successfully bridges modality gaps in OVSS and offers improved segmentation results, releasing resources for further research and development.

title: SECURE: Stable Early Collision Understanding via Robust Embeddings in Autonomous Driving
summary: While deep learning has significantly advanced accident anticipation, the robustness of these safety-critical systems against real-world perturbations remains a major challenge. We reveal that state-of-the-art models like CRASH, despite their high performance, exhibit significant instability in predictions and latent representations when faced with minor input perturbations, posing serious reliability risks. To address this, we introduce SECURE - Stable Early Collision Understanding Robust Embeddings, a framework that formally defines and enforces model robustness. SECURE is founded on four key attributes: consistency and stability in both prediction space and latent feature space. We propose a principled training methodology that fine-tunes a baseline model using a multi-objective loss, which minimizes divergence from a reference model and penalizes sensitivity to adversarial perturbations. Experiments on DAD and CCD datasets demonstrate that our approach not only significantly enhances robustness against various perturbations but also improves performance on clean data, achieving new state-of-the-art results.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces SECURE, a framework aimed at improving the robustness and stability of accident anticipation models using a novel multi-objective loss. Experiments show enhanced reliability and performance.
AI.motivation: Deep learning models for accident anticipation, such as CRASH, show vulnerability to perturbations, raising reliability concerns.
AI.method: SECURE employs a multi-objective loss to ensure consistency in prediction and latent spaces, using training that penalizes sensitivity and aligns with a reference model.
AI.result: Experiments on DAD and CCD datasets prove that SECURE improves robustness against perturbations while achieving state-of-the-art performance on unchanged data.
AI.conclusion: SECURE significantly boosts robustness and performance of accident anticipation models, addressing their prior instability issues.

title: Distributed Variational Quantum Linear Solver
summary: This paper develops a distributed variational quantum algorithm for solving large-scale linear equations. For a linear system of the form $Ax=b$, the large square matrix $A$ is partitioned into smaller square block submatrices, each of which is known only to a single noisy intermediate-scale quantum (NISQ) computer. Each NISQ computer communicates with certain other quantum computers in the same row and column of the block partition, where the communication patterns are described by the row- and column-neighbor graphs, both of which are connected. The proposed algorithm integrates a variant of the variational quantum linear solver at each computer with distributed classical optimization techniques. The derivation of the quantum cost function provides insight into the design of the distributed algorithm. Numerical quantum simulations demonstrate that the proposed distributed quantum algorithm can solve linear systems whose size scales with the number of computers and is therefore not limited by the capacity of a single quantum computer.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a distributed variational quantum algorithm for solving large linear equations by partitioning data and leveraging multiple NISQ computers connected through specific communication patterns.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the limitations of NISQ computers, which cannot handle large-scale computations alone, by distributing the workload across multiple devices.
AI.method: The method involves partitioning a large matrix into smaller submatrices, assigning each to a NISQ computer, and employing a variant of variational quantum solvers along with distributed classical optimization techniques.
AI.result: Numerical quantum simulations showed that the algorithm can handle linear systems of sizes proportional to the number of computers, overcoming single-device constraints.
AI.conclusion: The proposed distributed quantum algorithm efficiently leverages multiple NISQ devices for large-scale linear equations, offering a scalable solution beyond the limitations of individual quantum computers.

title: A Multi-Agent Human-LLM Collaborative Framework for Closed-Loop Scientific Literature Summarization
summary: Scientific discovery is slowed by fragmented literature that requires excessive human effort to gather, analyze, and understand. AI tools, including autonomous summarization and question answering, have been developed to aid in understanding scientific literature.
